modules.name: email.mime.application
documentation: Class representing application/* type MIME documents.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Keith Dart
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.application.MIMEApplication

methods.name: email.mime.application.MIMEApplication.__init__
documentation:
Create an application/* type MIME document.

_data is a string containing the raw applicatoin data.

_subtype is the MIME content type subtype, defaulting to
'octet-stream'.

_encoder is a function which will perform the actual encoding for
transport of the application data, defaulting to base64 encoding.

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to the base class
constructor, which turns them into parameters on the Content-Type
header.
Class for generating application/* MIME documents.

modules.name: email.mime.text
documentation: Class representing text/* type MIME documents.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.text.MIMEText

methods.name: email.mime.text.MIMEText.__init__
documentation:
Create a text/* type MIME document.

_text is the string for this message object.

_subtype is the MIME sub content type, defaulting to "plain".

_charset is the character set parameter added to the Content-Type
header.  This defaults to "us-ascii".  Note that as a side-effect, the
Content-Transfer-Encoding header will also be set.
Class for generating text/* type MIME documents.

modules.name: email.mime.image
documentation: Class representing image/* type MIME documents.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.image.MIMEImage

methods.name: email.mime.image.MIMEImage.__init__
documentation:
Create an image/* type MIME document.

_imagedata is a string containing the raw image data.  If this data
can be decoded by the standard Python `imghdr' module, then the
subtype will be automatically included in the Content-Type header.
Otherwise, you can specify the specific image subtype via the _subtype
parameter.

_encoder is a function which will perform the actual encoding for
transport of the image data.  It takes one argument, which is this
Image instance.  It should use get_payload() and set_payload() to
change the payload to the encoded form.  It should also add any
Content-Transfer-Encoding or other headers to the message as
necessary.  The default encoding is Base64.

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to the base class
constructor, which turns them into parameters on the Content-Type
header.
Class for generating image/* type MIME documents.

modules.name: email.mime.nonmultipart
documentation: Base class for MIME type messages that are not multipart.
Copyright (C) 2002-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.nonmultipart.MIMENonMultipart
documentation: Base class for MIME multipart/* type messages.

modules.name: email.mime.message
documentation: Class representing message/* MIME documents.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.message.MIMEMessage

methods.name: email.mime.message.MIMEMessage.__init__
documentation:
Create a message/* type MIME document.

_msg is a message object and must be an instance of Message, or a
derived class of Message, otherwise a TypeError is raised.

Optional _subtype defines the subtype of the contained message.  The
default is "rfc822" (this is defined by the MIME standard, even though
the term "rfc822" is technically outdated by RFC 2822).
Class representing message/* MIME documents.

modules.name: email.mime.multipart
documentation: Base class for MIME multipart/* type messages.
Copyright (C) 2002-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.mime.multipart.MIMEMultipart

methods.name: email.mime.multipart.MIMEMultipart.__init__
documentation:
Creates a multipart/* type message.

By default, creates a multipart/mixed message, with proper
Content-Type and MIME-Version headers.

_subtype is the subtype of the multipart content type, defaulting to
`mixed'.

boundary is the multipart boundary string.  By default it is
calculated as needed.

_subparts is a sequence of initial subparts for the payload.  It
must be an iterable object, such as a list.  You can always
attach new subparts to the message by using the attach() method.

Additional parameters for the Content-Type header are taken from the
keyword arguments (or passed into the _params argument).
Base class for MIME multipart/* type messages.

modules.name: email.mime.audio
documentation: Class representing audio/* type MIME documents.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Anthony Baxter
Contact: email-sig@python.org

functions.name: email.mime.audio._whatsnd
documentation:
Try to identify a sound file type.

sndhdr.what() has a pretty cruddy interface, unfortunately.  This is why
we re-do it here.  It would be easier to reverse engineer the Unix 'file'
command and use the standard 'magic' file, as shipped with a modern Unix.
There are others in sndhdr that don't have MIME types. :(
Additional ones to be added to sndhdr? midi, mp3, realaudio, wma??

classes.name: email.mime.audio.MIMEAudio

methods.name: email.mime.audio.MIMEAudio.__init__
documentation:
Create an audio/* type MIME document.

_audiodata is a string containing the raw audio data.  If this data
can be decoded by the standard Python `sndhdr' module, then the
subtype will be automatically included in the Content-Type header.
Otherwise, you can specify  the specific audio subtype via the
_subtype parameter.  If _subtype is not given, and no subtype can be
guessed, a TypeError is raised.

_encoder is a function which will perform the actual encoding for
transport of the image data.  It takes one argument, which is this
Image instance.  It should use get_payload() and set_payload() to
change the payload to the encoded form.  It should also add any
Content-Transfer-Encoding or other headers to the message as
necessary.  The default encoding is Base64.

Any additional keyword arguments are passed to the base class
constructor, which turns them into parameters on the Content-Type
header.
Class for generating audio/* MIME documents.

modules.name: email.utils
documentation: Miscellaneous utilities.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

functions.name: email.utils._identity
documentation: Helpers

functions.name: email.utils.fix_eols
documentation:
Replace all line-ending characters with 
.

functions.name: email.utils.formataddr
documentation:
The inverse of parseaddr(), this takes a 2-tuple of the form
(realname, email_address) and returns the string value suitable
for an RFC 2822 From, To or Cc header.

If the first element of pair is false, then the second element is
returned unmodified.

functions.name: email.utils.getaddresses
documentation: Return a list of (REALNAME, EMAIL) for each fieldvalue.

functions.name: email.utils.formatdate
documentation:
Returns a date string as specified by RFC 2822, e.g.:

Fri, 09 Nov 2001 01:08:47 -0000

Optional timeval if given is a floating point time value as accepted by
gmtime() and localtime(), otherwise the current time is used.

Optional localtime is a flag that when True, interprets timeval, and
returns a date relative to the local timezone instead of UTC, properly
taking daylight savings time into account.

Optional argument usegmt means that the timezone is written out as
an ascii string, not numeric one (so "GMT" instead of "+0000"). This
is needed for HTTP, and is only used when localtime==False.

functions.name: email.utils.make_msgid
documentation:
Returns a string suitable for RFC 2822 compliant Message-ID, e.g:

<20020201195627.33539.96671@nightshade.la.mastaler.com>

Optional idstring if given is a string used to strengthen the
uniqueness of the message id.

functions.name: email.utils.parsedate
documentation: These functions are in the standalone mimelib version only because they've
subsequently been fixed in the latest Python versions.  We use this to worm
around broken older Pythons.

functions.name: email.utils.unquote
documentation: Remove quotes from a string.
rfc822.unquote() doesn't properly de-backslash-ify in Python pre-2.3.

functions.name: email.utils.decode_rfc2231
documentation: Decode string according to RFC 2231
RFC2231-related functions - parameter encoding and decoding

functions.name: email.utils.encode_rfc2231
documentation:
Encode string according to RFC 2231.

If neither charset nor language is given, then s is returned as-is.  If
charset is given but not language, the string is encoded using the empty
string for language.

functions.name: email.utils.decode_params
documentation:
Decode parameters list according to RFC 2231.

params is a sequence of 2-tuples containing (param name, string value).

documentation: A package for parsing, handling, and generating email messages.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

functions.name: email.message_from_string
documentation:
Parse a string into a Message object model.

Optional _class and strict are passed to the Parser constructor.
Some convenience routines.  Don't import Parser and Message as side-effects
of importing email since those cascadingly import most of the rest of the
email package.

functions.name: email.message_from_file
documentation:
Read a file and parse its contents into a Message object model.

Optional _class and strict are passed to the Parser constructor.

modules.name: email.iterators
documentation: Various types of useful iterators and generators.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

functions.name: email.iterators.walk
documentation:
Walk over the message tree, yielding each subpart.

The walk is performed in depth-first order.  This method is a
generator.
This function will become a method of the Message class

functions.name: email.iterators.body_line_iterator
documentation:
Iterate over the parts, returning string payloads line-by-line.

Optional decode (default False) is passed through to .get_payload().
These two functions are imported into the Iterators.py interface module.

functions.name: email.iterators.typed_subpart_iterator
documentation:
Iterate over the subparts with a given MIME type.

Use `maintype' as the main MIME type to match against; this defaults to
"text".  Optional `subtype' is the MIME subtype to match against; if
omitted, only the main type is matched.

functions.name: email.iterators._structure
documentation: A handy debugging aid

modules.name: email.parser
documentation: A parser of RFC 2822 and MIME email messages.
Copyright (C) 2001-2006 Python Software Foundation
Author: Barry Warsaw, Thomas Wouters, Anthony Baxter
Contact: email-sig@python.org

methods.name: email.parser.Parser.__init__
documentation:
Parser of RFC 2822 and MIME email messages.

Creates an in-memory object tree representing the email message, which
can then be manipulated and turned over to a Generator to return the
textual representation of the message.

The string must be formatted as a block of RFC 2822 headers and header
continuation lines, optionally preceeded by a `Unix-from' header.  The
header block is terminated either by the end of the string or by a
blank line.

_class is the class to instantiate for new message objects when they
must be created.  This class must have a constructor that can take
zero arguments.  Default is Message.Message.

methods.name: email.parser.Parser.parse
documentation:
Create a message structure from the data in a file.

Reads all the data from the file and returns the root of the message
structure.  Optional headersonly is a flag specifying whether to stop
parsing after reading the headers or not.  The default is False,
meaning it parses the entire contents of the file.

methods.name: email.parser.Parser.parsestr
documentation:
Create a message structure from a string.

Returns the root of the message structure.  Optional headersonly is a
flag specifying whether to stop parsing after reading the headers or
not.  The default is False, meaning it parses the entire contents of
the file.

modules.name: email._parseaddr
documentation:
Email address parsing code.

Lifted directly from rfc822.py.  This should eventually be rewritten.
Copyright (C) 2002-2007 Python Software Foundation
Contact: email-sig@python.org

functions.name: email._parseaddr.parsedate_tz
documentation:
Convert a date string to a time tuple.

Accounts for military timezones.

functions.name: email._parseaddr.parsedate
documentation: Convert a time string to a time tuple.

functions.name: email._parseaddr.mktime_tz
documentation: Turn a 10-tuple as returned by parsedate_tz() into a UTC timestamp.

functions.name: email._parseaddr.quote
documentation: Add quotes around a string.

classes.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a new instance.

`field' is an unparsed address header field, containing
one or more addresses.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.gotonext
documentation: Parse up to the start of the next address.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getaddrlist
documentation:
Parse all addresses.

Returns a list containing all of the addresses.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getaddress
documentation: Parse the next address.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getrouteaddr
documentation:
Parse a route address (Return-path value).

This method just skips all the route stuff and returns the addrspec.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getaddrspec
documentation: Parse an RFC 2822 addr-spec.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getdomain
documentation: Get the complete domain name from an address.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getdelimited
documentation:
Parse a header fragment delimited by special characters.

`beginchar' is the start character for the fragment.
If self is not looking at an instance of `beginchar' then
getdelimited returns the empty string.

`endchars' is a sequence of allowable end-delimiting characters.
Parsing stops when one of these is encountered.

If `allowcomments' is non-zero, embedded RFC 2822 comments are allowed
within the parsed fragment.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getquote
documentation: Get a quote-delimited fragment from self's field.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getcomment
documentation: Get a parenthesis-delimited fragment from self's field.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getdomainliteral
documentation: Parse an RFC 2822 domain-literal.

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getatom
documentation:
Parse an RFC 2822 atom.

Optional atomends specifies a different set of end token delimiters
(the default is to use self.atomends).  This is used e.g. in
getphraselist() since phrase endings must not include the `.' (which
is legal in phrases).

methods.name: email._parseaddr.AddrlistClass.getphraselist
documentation:
Parse a sequence of RFC 2822 phrases.

A phrase is a sequence of words, which are in turn either RFC 2822
atoms or quoted-strings.  Phrases are canonicalized by squeezing all
runs of continuous whitespace into one space.

Address parser class by Ben Escoto.

To understand what this class does, it helps to have a copy of RFC 2822 in
front of you.

Note: this class interface is deprecated and may be removed in the future.
Use rfc822.AddressList instead.

classes.name: email._parseaddr.AddressList
documentation: An AddressList encapsulates a list of parsed RFC 2822 addresses.

modules.name: email.feedparser
documentation:
FeedParser - An email feed parser.

The feed parser implements an interface for incrementally parsing an email
message, line by line.  This has advantages for certain applications, such as
those reading email messages off a socket.

FeedParser.feed() is the primary interface for pushing new data into the
parser.  It returns when there's nothing more it can do with the available
data.  When you have no more data to push into the parser, call .close().
This completes the parsing and returns the root message object.

The other advantage of this parser is that it will never throw a parsing
exception.  Instead, when it finds something unexpected, it adds a 'defect' to
the current message.  Defects are just instances that live on the message
object's .defects attribute.
Copyright (C) 2004-2006 Python Software Foundation
Authors: Baxter, Wouters and Warsaw
Contact: email-sig@python.org

classes.name: email.feedparser.BufferedSubFile

methods.name: email.feedparser.BufferedSubFile.push
documentation: Push some new data into this object.

A file-ish object that can have new data loaded into it.

You can also push and pop line-matching predicates onto a stack.  When the
current predicate matches the current line, a false EOF response
(i.e. empty string) is returned instead.  This lets the parser adhere to a
simple abstraction -- it parses until EOF closes the current message.

classes.name: email.feedparser.FeedParser

methods.name: email.feedparser.FeedParser.__init__
documentation: _factory is called with no arguments to create a new message obj

methods.name: email.feedparser.FeedParser._set_headersonly
documentation: Non-public interface for supporting Parser's headersonly flag

methods.name: email.feedparser.FeedParser.feed
documentation: Push more data into the parser.

methods.name: email.feedparser.FeedParser.close
documentation: Parse all remaining data and return the root message object.
A feed-style parser of email.
